Um, so tena koutou katoa, chair and members of the Environment Select
Committee. I'm Richard McIntyre, and I'm a dairy farmer and farmer-elected
director of Dairy NZ.
With me today is David Cooper, who you've just talked to before.
He's our principal policy advisor.
Dairy NZ is the industry good organization representing all New
Zealand, all New Zealand dairy farmers.
We are funded by a levy on milk solids and exist to help farmers build
profitable, sustainable, resilient businesses through
extension, advocacy, science and
research. Dairy is one of New Zealand's largest export
earners, with twenty-seven billion dollars in export earnings
in the year to June 2025, representing
roughly one in every four export dollars.
The sector directly employs around twenty-four thousand people,
increasing to an estimated fifty-five thousand people if you include
processing and farm support services.
As a farmer, I've seen firsthand how the sector has stepped
up. Over the last decade, dairy farmers have widely adopted
practices like waterway fencing, improved effluent management,
nutrient budgeting and farm environment plans through the Sustainable
Dairy and Water Accord and Dairy Tomorrow initiatives.
Today, more than ninety-eight percent of significant waterways on dairy farms are
fenced, so that's over twenty-four thousand kilometers, and over eighty-four
percent of farms operate under industry-led
farm environment plans.
Recent research estimates that those dairy farms who have
implemented industry-driven good farming practices have seen nitrogen
losses drop by twenty percent and phosphorus losses drop by
fourteen percent between 2015 and 2023.
Dairy NZ supports resource management reform and the government's goal of
enabling primary sector growth while maintaining or improving environmental
outcomes. These goals are
compatible. However, as drafted, the bill falls
short of delivering on ministerial intent.
Our submission provides detailed redrafting that aligns with the structure of the
bills. Because many amendments are
interconnected, they should be considered as a package of
changes. We have been working across the primary
sector to ensure alignment. I'll now pass to David to
outline our priority changes.
Kia ora koutou. Uh, so primary industries underpin New Zealand's social and
economic well-being, as Richard has said, and rely on access to natural
resources. The bills must explicitly support sustainable resource
use within environmental limits.
So we've proposed two key amendments to the goals of the Natural Environment Bill.
First is explicit recognition of primary production as a nationally significant
activity. Second is a clear statement enabling resource
use, responsible resource use, subject to clauses
11B and 11C of the goals. Our concern is in the absence
of this recognition, the Act will be implemented in a way that risks placing too
little weight on the benefits of agriculture or resource use as the
Act is applied, imposing a hierarchy.
Clause 11B of the goals requires safeguard, safeguarding the life-supporting
capacity of air, water, soil and ecosystems, and Clause
11C requires protection of human health.
The goals need an explicit recognition of the importance of resource use and the
importance of primary production.
Without this recognition, if the goals were applied to the extent we are
concerned, the implications would be highly detrimental to the future of
agriculture and, rural and the national communities.
We also propose additional guiding principles to better bridge the gap
between high-level policy intent and the practical
implementation. These additional principles act as
guardrails to ensure the system delivers the government's intended
outcomes. They would build on the existing principles by
embedding an enable where possible approach while being subject to
meeting environmental goals. Dairy NZ
supports setting limits on natural resource use.
For fresh water, it is essential to amend the NEB,
to ensure there is a clear distinction between human and ecosystem health
outcomes. That is, national human health limits with
clear numerical thresholds, regional environmental
limits that may also be numerical, but equally allow for
qualitative direction of travel approaches where numerical limits are not
reliable. Providing for a qualitative approach is
essential when setting regional environmental limits.
Catchments are complex natural environments.
We don't yet have the tools to translate rigid catchment-level
numerical limits into accurate evidence-based requirements at the
farm scale. This creates a false sense of
precision and risks ineffective or disproportionate
regulation. A more flexible qualitative framework would better
support and deliver practical ecosystem health outcomes,
outcomes. The level at which numerical limits are set
can fundamentally determine where-- whether farming can continue as a
catchmentAs an example, the Maungawhenuku catchment in Tukituki
in Hawke's Bay, modeling of nitrate shows that to get above what
has been regarded as the national bottom line, that is the bottom of C band, you
would need to convert all dairy land around three th-- three thousand hectares
plus approximately twenty thousand hectares of sheep and beef to
forestry. This would reduce sheep and beef from seventy-nine
percent of the catchment to around twenty percent of the catchment.
If you wanted to be more ambitious and aim for the next band, B band, you would
need to remove all pastoral agriculture, and even then the catchment
would still not reach B band minimum bottom lines.
These results illustrate that pursuing B band outcomes in these catchments would
require land use change. The required reduction in diffuse
nutrient losses would be far beyond what is feasible through on-farm
practice improvements or mitigation.
This is on an isolated case highlighting the need for careful proportionate limit
setting to avoid disproportionate land use impacts.
When setting limits, understanding these potential costs is critical, but the bill
does not require that. We think that leaves existing gearing and other foreign
production highly vuller-- vulnerable.
So we recommend that legislation, A, allows for qualitative limits when
numerical limits are inappropriate, B, requires consideration
of current water quality and land use patterns, and C, requires
transparent justification reports with cost benefit and risk
asse-assessments to both inform commu-- regulators and also
enable for that, informed community engagement on trade-offs and what
we're trying to achieve.
So these changes will guide workable secondary legislation
and avoid repeating the unachievable
limits.
Fantastic. So farm plans are already being used to make decisions in the
dairy sector. On the farms we are responsible for,
the plan helps us identify where the key risks are and guides our actions and
investment to the areas that will make the biggest difference in terms of reducing
environmental footprint. That's true across the sector, with
farmers using farm plans as a tool for consistent improvement
in ways that fit their land and systems.
The NEB provides an opportunity to build on farm plans as an
effective tool. Dairy farmers wanna see freshwater farm plans
triggering permitted activity status where those activities are best
managed through a freshwater farm plan.
As drafted, it is not clear they replace existing rules.
In summary, we are seeking amendments to ensure certified and
auditable freshwater farm plans are the primary tool for managing
on-farm environmental risk. Limit the ability to
create duplicate rules and regulations.
If the legislation recognizes farm plans as a core
mechanism, we get a system that is practical, targeted
and effective. This, this can embed continuous improvement
rather than layering on added impractical and unnecessary
cost. I'll pass to David now to discuss permitted activities.
And we support the government's aim of reducing unnecessary consents and increasing
permitted activities. As drafted, the bills, we do not believe achieve
this. Permitted activities are an efficient way to manage low impact
anticipated farm activities supported by freshwater farm plans, national
standards, and targeted regulations or actions where needed.
If these tools can ensure activities do not have a significant impact on the
ability to meet limits, and in fact, they can ensure progress is made
towards limits that are already exceeded, they should be permitted.
Yet under the current drafting, even routine activities such as grazing or track
maintenance would require consent in catchments that are considered to be in breach
of limits. So that is highly inefficient and co-contrary to
the government's intent. To amend this, we're seeking
removal of mandatory registration permitted activities.
Although, registration should be available, but not as a
default. Clear permitted activity pathways, including
catchments at or near limits, provided the package of regulations will
result in improvement over
time. Permitted activities
ex-- supported by what is a, a bit of an intervention
framework in clauses sixty to sixty-seven.
Again, we believe this drafting requires refinement.
Action plans appear intended to draw on existing catchment and community-led
work. Yet as drafted, they primarily
function as regulatory tools. So we're seeking amendments to ensure
action plans remain collaborative, non-regulatory mechanisms that
communities can meaningfully shape and own.
Farmers want the efforts to be recognized before being, before more
stringent interventions imposed, but they do not want, want to
become de facto regulators, through action plans.
Those clauses also in-introduce, what we're calling an intervention
sequencing, where the framework also
requires... The framework requires decision-makers to consider lower
costs, enabling regulatory and non-regulatory options before escalating to
more stringent measures. The sequencing is essential
to ensure that the system u-uses the least cost, most
eff-effectively tools first, as the first
levers before regulators start pulling those more stringent
levers. Uh, back to you, Richard.
Right. So in closing, the bill requires a package of integrated amendments to
deliver a durable system that provides regulatory certainty for
primary production while achieving better economic, social and
environmental outcomes for all New Zealanders.
These integrated changes will deliver the government's intent
for a system that enables sustainable primary production while achieving
positive environmental outcomes.
Enduring legislation is essential.
There needs to be a provision for use of natural resources in a sustainable
manner. Farmers have faced repeated shifts in national
direction, triggering costly and contentious processes that
have consumed tens, possibly hundreds of millions of dollars.
Resources that would have been far better invested in practical environmental
action.Dairy NZ is committed to constructive engagement
through the select committee process
and on secondary legislation. We remain available to workshop
drafting and implementation details.
Thank you, and we welcome questions.

economic framing is wrong and must shift

But if we consider the atmosphere's limited capacity to absorb
greenhouse gas
and the rising risk of irreversible warming, the assumption

close relationship between GDP and
environmental impact, why would we,
why would we prioritize accelerating
growth? The alternative
is to heed
the evidence that we are hitting environmental limits
and, as a priority, legislate to preserve and restore

shaped by ideology.

nature to sustain us-
